section.title: Double integrals over general regions
section.p: A rectangle is easy to slice because every vertical slice has the same y-limits, and every horizontal slice has the same x-limits. A general region asks for more care.
The main rule is not a formula. It is a habit:
section.md: \boxed{\text{Draw the region before writing the integral.}}
section.p: The picture tells us the limits.

subsection.title: A triangular flower bed
subsection.p: A triangular flower bed is bounded by y=0, \qquad x=4, \qquad y=\frac{x}{2}. Suppose the height of a layer of mulch spread over the bed is modeled by m(x,y)=6-y. Find the total volume of mulch over the bed.
The region is D=\left\{(x,y):0\le x\le4,\ 0\le y\le \frac{x}{2}\right\}. For each fixed x, the vertical slice runs from the bottom edge y=0 to the slanted edge y=\frac{x}{2}.

figure.caption: For this triangle, a vertical slice has variable height x/2.

subsection.p:
Therefore V=\iint_D (6-y)\,dA becomes V=\int_0^4\int_0^{x/2} (6-y)\,dy\,dx. Compute the inner integral:
\int_0^{x/2} (6-y)\,dy \amp= \left[6y-\frac12y^2\right]_0^{x/2} \amp= 3x-\frac{x^2}{8}.
Now integrate in x:
V \amp= \int_0^4 \left( 3x-\frac{x^2}{8} \right)\,dx \amp= \left[ \frac32x^2-\frac{x^3}{24} \right]_0^4 \amp= 24-\frac{64}{24} \amp= 24-\frac{8}{3} \amp= \frac{64}{3}.
Thus \boxed{V=\frac{64}{3}.}
The only new feature was the upper limit \frac{x}{2}. The upper edge of the region became a limit of integration.

subsection.title: Type I regions
subsection.p: The triangular flower bed is an example of a region that is easy to slice vertically.

statement.p: A region D in the plane is called a Type I region if it can be written as \boxed{ D= \left\{ (x,y): a\le x\le b,\ \phi_1(x)\le y\le \phi_2(x) \right\}. } Here the x-values run from a to b, and for each fixed x, the y-values run from a lower curve y=\phi_1(x) to an upper curve y=\phi_2(x).

subsection.p: For a Type I region, \boxed{ \iint_D f(x,y)\,dA = \int_a^b \int_{\phi_1(x)}^{\phi_2(x)} f(x,y)\,dy\,dx. }
The inner integral moves along a vertical slice. The outer integral moves those slices from left to right.

figure.caption: A Type I region is described by vertical slices.

theorem.title: Double integral over a Type I region

statement.p: Suppose D= \left\{ (x,y): a\le x\le b,\ \phi_1(x)\le y\le \phi_2(x) \right\}, where \phi_1 and \phi_2 are continuous, and suppose f is continuous on D. Then \boxed{ \iint_D f(x,y)\,dA = \int_a^b \int_{\phi_1(x)}^{\phi_2(x)} f(x,y)\,dy\,dx. }

proof.title: Idea of the proof
proof.p: For each fixed x, the vertical cross-section of D has y-values from \phi_1(x) to \phi_2(x). So the inner integral \int_{\phi_1(x)}^{\phi_2(x)} f(x,y)\,dy adds f along that vertical slice. The outer integral then adds all those slices from x=a to x=b.
This is the same slicing idea as Fubini's theorem, except now the slice endpoints can move with x.

subsection.title: Type II regions
subsection.p: Some regions are easier to slice horizontally.

statement.p: A region D in the plane is called a Type II region if it can be written as \boxed{ D= \left\{ (x,y): c\le y\le d,\ \psi_1(y)\le x\le \psi_2(y) \right\}. } Here the y-values run from c to d, and for each fixed y, the x-values run from a left curve x=\psi_1(y) to a right curve x=\psi_2(y).

subsection.p: For a Type II region, \boxed{ \iint_D f(x,y)\,dA = \int_c^d \int_{\psi_1(y)}^{\psi_2(y)} f(x,y)\,dx\,dy. }

figure.caption: A Type II region is described by horizontal slices.

theorem.title: Double integral over a Type II region

statement.p: Suppose D= \left\{ (x,y): c\le y\le d,\ \psi_1(y)\le x\le \psi_2(y) \right\}, where \psi_1 and \psi_2 are continuous, and suppose f is continuous on D. Then \boxed{ \iint_D f(x,y)\,dA = \int_c^d \int_{\psi_1(y)}^{\psi_2(y)} f(x,y)\,dx\,dy. }

subsection.title: Drawing the region before integrating
subsection.p: The hardest part of many double integral problems is not the integration. It is setting up the bounds.
A reliable routine is:

li.p: Decide whether vertical slices or horizontal slices are simpler.
Write the outer limits from the shadow of the region on the outer axis.
Write the inner limits from the slice.

subsection.p: The word shadow is useful. For a Type I setup, the region casts a shadow on the x-axis: a\le x\le b. For a Type II setup, it casts a shadow on the y-axis: c\le y\le d.

subsection.title: Reversing the order of integration
subsection.p: Sometimes one order leads to an integral we cannot evaluate with elementary functions. The other order may be easy.
Consider I= \int_0^1\int_y^1 e^{x^2}\,dx\,dy. The inner integral \int_y^1 e^{x^2}\,dx has no elementary antiderivative. But the region may save us.
The bounds say 0\le y\le1, \qquad y\le x\le1. So the region is the triangle 0\le y\le x\le1. Equivalently, 0\le x\le1, \qquad 0\le y\le x.

figure.caption: The integral \int_0^1\int_y^1 e^{x^2}\,dx\,dy is over the triangle 0\le y\le x\le1.

subsection.p: The integral became possible not because the function changed, but because the slices changed.

subsection.title: A region that must be split
subsection.p: Not every region is Type I or Type II in one piece. Sometimes a slice hits the region in two separate intervals. Then one pair of limits would fill in space that is not really part of the region.
For example, let D= \bigl([0,1]\times[0,1]\bigr) \cup \bigl([0,1]\times[2,3]\bigr). This is two square islands with a gap between them.

A vertical slice at a fixed x hits 0\le y\le1 and also 2\le y\le3. It does not hit the gap 1\lt y\lt 2. So writing 0\le x\le1, \qquad 0\le y\le3 would describe the full rectangle, not the two islands.
The correct setup is a sum: \boxed{ \iint_D f(x,y)\,dA = \int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dy\,dx + \int_0^1\int_2^3 f(x,y)\,dy\,dx. }
The principle is simple:
subsection.md: \boxed{\text{If one slice breaks into pieces, split the region.}}

subsection.title: Regions with holes
subsection.p: A hole is another reason to split or subtract.
Imagine an L-shaped floor: D= [0,3]\times[0,2] \setminus [1,3]\times[1,2]. It is a 3-by-2 rectangle with the upper-right rectangle removed.

Suppose f(x,y)=1+x is the density of a thin coating on the floor. The total amount of coating is \iint_D (1+x)\,dA. One method is to split the L-shape into two rectangles: D_1=[0,3]\times[0,1], and D_2=[0,1]\times[1,2]. Then \iint_D (1+x)\,dA = \int_0^3\int_0^1 (1+x)\,dy\,dx + \int_0^1\int_1^2 (1+x)\,dy\,dx. Compute:
\int_0^3\int_0^1 (1+x)\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^3 (1+x)\,dx \amp= \left[x+\frac12x^2\right]_0^3 \amp= 3+\frac92 \amp= \frac{15}{2}.
Also,
\int_0^1\int_1^2 (1+x)\,dy\,dx \amp= \int_0^1 (1+x)\,dx \amp= \left[x+\frac12x^2\right]_0^1 \amp= \frac32.
Therefore \boxed{ \iint_D (1+x)\,dA = \frac{15}{2}+\frac32 = 9. }

figure.caption: An L-shaped region is easiest to integrate over by splitting it into pieces.

subsection.p: Another method is subtraction: \iint_D (1+x)\,dA = \iint_{[0,3]\times[0,2]}(1+x)\,dA - \iint_{[1,3]\times[1,2]}(1+x)\,dA. The full rectangle contributes \int_0^3\int_0^2(1+x)\,dy\,dx = 2\int_0^3(1+x)\,dx = 15. The removed rectangle contributes \int_1^3\int_1^2(1+x)\,dy\,dx = \int_1^3(1+x)\,dx = 6. So again 15-6=9.
Both methods are correct. The good method is the one that makes the region easiest to describe.

subsection.title: The main lesson
subsection.p: For rectangles, the limits are constant: a\le x\le b, \qquad c\le y\le d. For general regions, one pair of limits often depends on the other variable: \phi_1(x)\le y\le\phi_2(x) or \psi_1(y)\le x\le\psi_2(y).
So the calculation is still based on one idea:
The only difference is that the slices may grow, shrink, split, or avoid holes.
The next obstacle is circular geometry. A disk can be described with x- and y-bounds, but the limits contain square roots. A circular ring is even less friendly in rectangular coordinates.
For circular regions, the natural slices are not vertical or horizontal. They are radial and angular.
That leads to polar coordinates.
